metadata.title: PartyThrow Tech Diligence Storyline
metadata.author: KPMG LLP
metadata.subject: Mid-size SaaS commercial and financial diligence

slides.title: PartyThrow Tech: Mid-Market SaaS Diligence
slides.subtitle: Management diligence storyline for simulated EdTech target, period covered January 2023 to December 2025

sections.title: Executive Summary
items: What the numbers say about investment quality
Why growth quality is good but not risk free

sections.title: Market and Product
items: Positioning in EdTech SaaS and packaging model

sections.title: Growth Quality
items: Net new MRR durability and churn pressure

sections.title: Unit Economics
items: Margins, operating leverage, and cash conversion

slides.title: Revenue scaled 27.5% CAGR with margin resilience
slides.strapline: Top-line expansion and stable gross margin indicate a scalable software model, with operating margin recovery visible in 2025.
body: Revenue expanded from $64.7M in 2023 to $105.1M in 2025, implying 27.5% CAGR across the observed period and sustained demand momentum.
Gross margin remained 73.2% in each year, suggesting limited gross margin dilution as the company scaled customer and ARR volume.
Operating margin dipped to 13.2% in 2024 during growth investment then recovered to 14.9% in 2025, indicating emerging operating discipline.
Implication: the baseline case supports a quality-growth narrative if churn and working-capital volatility are contained in underwriting assumptions.

slides.title: Investment case is attractive with clear watchouts
slides.strapline: The core thesis is growth at scale with strong software margins, balanced by concentration of execution risks in churn and QoE items.
insights: Core strengths support the investment case, but quality of growth remains sensitive to retention outcomes and EBITDA normalization assumptions.
Underwriting should include churn downside and explicit QoE adjustments before valuation and leverage are finalized.

slides.title: EdTech positioning supports continued expansion
slides.strapline: The company is positioned as a mid-market cloud platform with a credible path to grow share through product depth and enterprise motion.
body: PartyThrow Tech operates in the EdTech software segment where buyers prioritize reliability, integration quality, and measurable learning outcomes.
Management has built a base of 1,834 customers with growth milestones since launch, indicating product-market fit beyond early adopter cohorts.
Current positioning is strongest in mid-market accounts where the platform offers meaningful functionality at lower complexity than large-suite alternatives.
Commercial momentum suggests room to move up-market if implementation quality and customer success coverage stay aligned to higher-contract accounts.
Market narrative supports upside, but diligence should validate win-loss dynamics and pipeline conversion assumptions by segment before value creation planning.

slides.title: Three-tier packaging supports land-and-expand
slides.strapline: Plan architecture offers clear entry, growth, and enterprise tiers, which supports segmentation and staged upsell motions.
slides.heading: Current packaging and list pricing
body: Starter, Professional, and Enterprise plans create a natural progression path that can support both SMB acquisition and upmarket expansion.
Current active subscriptions skew to enterprise count and MRR in the simulated dataset, which boosts scale but can raise concentration sensitivity.
Average monthly prices of $99, $299, and $999 create clear value ladders, making packaging understandable for sales and finance forecasting.
Implication: value creation should focus on expansion playbooks and retention controls to protect enterprise-heavy recurring revenue streams.

slides.title: ARR reached $135.2M with sustained momentum
slides.strapline: Year-end recurring revenue increased each year, supporting a durable growth narrative at the topline recurring level.
body: Year-end ARR increased from $80.5M in 2023 to $101.2M in 2024 and $135.2M in 2025, reflecting broad recurring base expansion.
The largest absolute increase occurred in 2025, showing acceleration despite known churn events and prior investment-cycle variability.
Recurring revenue growth remained ahead of fixed overhead growth, which supports the case for margin resilience over the medium term.
Implication: valuation can anchor on recurring scale, but diligence should test retention durability and cohort behavior by plan tier.

slides.title: Customer base tripled as ARPU normalized
slides.strapline: Scale came from a large increase in active customers, with expected ARPU normalization as the company broadened into lower-ticket cohorts.
body: Active customers increased from 562 at 2023 year-end to 1,649 at 2025 year-end, which demonstrates strong customer acquisition throughput.
Average MRR per active customer declined from $11.9k to $6.8k over the same period as mix broadened across plans and newer cohorts.
This mix shift is not inherently negative, but it raises the importance of disciplined upsell and retention motions to protect lifetime value.
Implication: growth quality remains favorable if future bookings maintain healthy cohort retention and expansion rates post-onboarding.

slides.title: Net new MRR stayed positive despite churn
slides.strapline: Quarterly acquisition and expansion generally outpaced churn, though churn volatility remains a material diligence watchpoint.
body: Across quarter-end snapshots, new plus expansion MRR exceeded churn in each period, producing positive net new recurring contribution.
Churn spikes are visible in multiple quarters and align with verification flags that identify seven customer losses totaling about $8.0M impact.
Growth durability therefore depends on reducing preventable churn while preserving expansion productivity across mature cohorts.
Implication: value creation should prioritize customer health scoring, renewal process rigor, and at-risk account intervention capacity.

slides.title: Gross profit expanded with stable unit economics
slides.strapline: Consistent gross margin and rising gross profit indicate favorable unit economics at current product and support cost structure.
body: Gross profit increased from $47.3M in 2023 to $76.9M in 2025 while gross margin remained flat at 73.2% throughout the period.
This pattern indicates that incremental revenue was not purchased through structural COGS inflation, supporting confidence in SaaS cost architecture.
As scale grows, stable gross margin provides room to absorb controlled GTM and product investments without impairing core economics.
Implication: management can prioritize growth initiatives while maintaining disciplined margin guardrails in annual planning cycles.

slides.title: Operating leverage is improving post-2024 dip
slides.strapline: Operating margin compression in 2024 appears temporary, with recovery in 2025 as revenue growth outpaced opex growth.
body: Operating income rose from $10.3M in 2023 to $15.6M in 2025 despite a temporary margin trough in 2024.
Operating margin moved from 15.9% to 13.2% then recovered to 14.9%, consistent with investment-led compression followed by scale efficiency.
Opex increased in absolute terms but remained manageable relative to recurring growth, supporting an improving leverage trajectory.
Implication: base-case underwriting can assume modest margin expansion if retention and GTM productivity are actively managed.

slides.title: Collections and NWC need tighter controls
slides.strapline: Aging quality is currently acceptable, but working-capital volatility signals the need for stronger forecasting and collections cadence.
insights: Aging buckets do not show severe delinquency today, but large receivables and volatile operating NWC can distort near-term cash conversion.
Diligence should stress-test collections assumptions, deferred revenue behavior, and quarter-end working-capital mechanics before close planning.

slides.title: Primary risks are identifiable and actionable
slides.strapline: Most material risks are concentrated in retention volatility and QoE normalization, both of which can be addressed through focused diligence.
leftBody: Customer churn risk: seven identified losses with aggregate impact around $8.0M indicate vulnerability in renewal and customer health coverage.
QoE adjustment complexity: ten flagged issues include one-time items and standard normalization topics that can change reported run-rate earnings.
Working-capital timing risk: quarter-end swings in operating NWC can pressure cash conversion and short-term leverage headroom.
rightBody: Retention program: launch top-account health scoring, executive renewal governance, and early-warning churn intervention in customer success.
QoE workstream: establish normalized EBITDA bridge with documented treatment for one-time events and owner-related adjustments.
Cash discipline: set weekly receivables and billing cadence controls with rolling 13-week cash visibility through integration planning.

slides.title: Next steps for diligence-to-value creation handoff
slides.strapline: The immediate objective is to close open diligence items while preparing a practical 100-day plan tied to retention, margin, and cash outcomes.
body: Complete QoE normalization with explicit treatment of one-time product launch effects and churn-driven revenue sensitivity scenarios.
Validate cohort retention and upsell assumptions by plan tier, including renewal timing and customer concentration in top enterprise accounts.
Reconcile working-capital mechanics to close model, with specific tests on AR collections and deferred revenue conversion behavior.
Stand up retention control tower, enforce renewal operating rhythm, and align sales-customer success incentives on net revenue outcomes.
Implement cost governance around growth investments while preserving product roadmap milestones tied to enterprise expansion.
Define integration KPI pack with monthly tracking for ARR, net new MRR, churn, gross margin, operating margin, and cash conversion.

slides.disclaimer: The information in this presentation is based on a simulated data room and is intended solely for diligence workflow testing. It does not represent investment advice, an audited opinion, or a recommendation to pursue any transaction.
